part_ii_sicknessillness_or_mentalnervous.describe_your_illness:
I was diagnosed with severe infectious mononucleosis (often called mono), complicated by acute viral hepatitis and prolonged post-viral fatigue. I first started feeling unusually tired and sore near the end of the fall semester but assumed it was just stress from finals and working a part-time job. Over winter break, my symptoms escalated significantly: I developed a persistent high fever, a very sore throat, swollen lymph nodes in my neck and underarms, and constant, deep fatigue that did not improve with rest.

When I returned to campus for the spring semester, I tried to push through the symptoms, but things got worse. I began experiencing dizziness when walking between classes, difficulty concentrating during lectures, and episodes of almost falling asleep in the library even after a full night of sleep. I also developed pain and a sense of fullness in the upper right side of my abdomen. After a campus clinic visit and subsequent lab work, my primary care physician back home confirmed that I had infectious mononucleosis caused by the Epstein-Barr virus, with associated liver inflammation (hepatitis) and markedly elevated liver enzymes.

Because of the liver involvement, I was instructed to avoid all physical exertion, including walking long distances on campus and participating in the intramural soccer team I had signed up for. I was also told to avoid common over-the-counter pain medications like acetaminophen in high doses, which made managing the fever and body aches more complicated. My doctor noted that my spleen was enlarged, which put me at risk for a splenic rupture if I fell or engaged in any contact or strenuous physical activity. I was advised to stay home, rest extensively, and avoid exposure to additional infections, as my immune system was compromised.

The combination of extreme fatigue, cognitive fog, and the need for near-constant rest made it impossible for me to keep up with the demands of full-time university coursework. I found that I could only remain awake and mentally focused for short periods—maybe 30–45 minutes at a time—before needing to lie down. Attempting to attend classes in person or even keep up with asynchronous online work quickly led to setbacks: my fever would spike, my throat would worsen, and I would feel completely drained for the rest of the day.

My treating physician has documented that this is not just a typical mild case of mono but a more severe presentation with organ involvement and a prolonged recovery trajectory. They have recommended that I withdraw from the current semester so that I can focus on recovery, avoid further complications, and have a realistic chance of returning to school in good health in a future term.
